company.name: Marathon Petroleum
company.description: Marathon Petroleum is a U.S. downstream energy company with refining, branded fuel marketing, renewable fuels, and MPLX-linked midstream operations.

overview.title: Business mix
paragraphs: Marathon Petroleum operates the largest U.S. refining system by capacity, with 13 refineries and roughly 3 million barrels per calendar day of crude oil refining capacity.
Its retail-facing fuel footprint is built around Marathon and ARCO branded locations, while its midstream exposure is primarily through MPLX LP.

overview.title: Current position
paragraphs: The company remains deeply tied to refining margins, crude differentials, logistics integration, and fuel distribution scale rather than a software-style network effect.
In 2025, MPC reported $4.0 billion of net income attributable to the company and continued returning capital through dividends and repurchases.

moatNarrative: Marathon Petroleum's moat is physical and operational: refinery scale, pipeline and terminal integration, branded fuel distribution, working-capital capability, regulatory experience, and advantaged access to refined-product logistics.

That moat is strong in the existing liquid-fuels economy, but it is exposed to structural substitution if distributed electricity, local renewables, demand flexibility, and vehicle electrification reduce the need for centralized refining and branded gasoline stops.
decentralizationNarrative: The core product is difficult to decentralize because refining, fuel logistics, safety compliance, and environmental regulation strongly favor large industrial operators.

The more credible decentralization pressure comes from replacing petroleum demand at the edge: open microgrid software, community-owned distributed energy, interoperable charging, and household or neighborhood renewable systems that reduce miles and gallons purchased from branded fuel networks.
company.snapshotNote: Queued from the May 25, 2026 S&P 500 market-cap snapshot ranks 151-175; refreshed with public company, market-data, and open-energy sources reviewed on 2026-05-29.

moat.rationale: Refining scale, logistics integration, branded retail reach, and MPLX-linked midstream assets create a durable incumbent position in liquid fuels.

decentralizability.rationale: Refining and petroleum distribution are capital-intensive and tightly regulated, but end-demand can be partly displaced by distributed energy systems and electrification.

profitability.rationale: MPC reported $4.0 billion of 2025 net income attributable to the company, though earnings remain cyclical and dependent on refining margins.

peRatio.rationale: CompaniesMarketCap reported a May 2026 trailing P/E ratio of about 16.4 for MPC.

marketCap.rationale: CompaniesMarketCap reported Marathon Petroleum's market capitalization at about $73.37 billion in May 2026, with a May 28, 2026 end-of-day figure near the same level.

products.name: Marathon and ARCO fuel
products.summary: Marathon and ARCO are MPC's consumer-facing fuel brands, spanning thousands of branded locations in the United States and northern Mexico.
products.whyItMatters: Branded gasoline and diesel retail is the public edge of MPC's refining and logistics system, where petroleum demand meets consumers, fleets, rewards programs, and fuel cards.
replacementSketch: The practical replacement path is not a drop-in open-source gasoline station. It is a gradual reduction in fuel dependence through local electricity generation, open energy management, interoperable charging, and community-scale coordination.

For fleets and households, the disruptive wedge is a stack that makes locally generated electricity and demand flexibility cheaper and more reliable than repeated purchases from centralized fuel distribution networks.

alternatives.name: OpenEMS
alternatives.summary: OpenEMS is an open-source energy management platform for coordinating renewable generation, storage, grid interaction, consumption, EV charging, heat pumps, and related devices.

openness.rationale: The project publishes open-source code and technical documentation and is organized around a public association and developer community.

decentralizationFit.rationale: OpenEMS helps coordinate edge energy assets such as batteries, EV chargers, renewable generation, and flexible loads, which supports localized energy substitution.

readiness.rationale: The project has documentation, a public repository, commercial adopters, and an active ecosystem, but deployment still requires hardware integration and energy-domain expertise.

costLeverage.rationale: Open controls can reduce vendor lock-in and improve dispatch of local assets, but the largest costs remain physical equipment, installation, interconnection, and maintenance.

alternatives.name: LF Energy Open Renewable Energy Systems
alternatives.summary: LF Energy's ORES initiative works on open specifications and an open-source stack for modular, interoperable, decentralized renewable energy systems down to household scale.

openness.rationale: ORES is positioned by LF Energy as an open-source working group and specification effort for renewable energy system components.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The project explicitly targets disaggregated renewable systems, household-level deployment, off-grid use, mini-grids, and bidirectional energy transmission.

readiness.rationale: ORES is more of an emerging ecosystem and working-group architecture than a mature drop-in product for replacing fuel retail today.

costLeverage.rationale: If standardized, interoperable components mature, they could lower integration costs for household and community renewable systems, but hardware costs and deployment complexity remain material.

disruptionConcepts.name: Community energy fuel displacement
disruptionConcepts.summary: A cooperative or municipal energy stack combines solar, storage, open energy management, and interoperable EV charging so households and fleets buy fewer gallons from branded fuel networks.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: The market structure shifts from centralized refining and branded retail distribution toward locally coordinated electricity production and flexible demand, reducing petroleum throughput at the consumer edge.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Decentralization matters through local ownership, open control surfaces, and multi-party microgrid coordination rather than through Bitcoin; participants can operate shared infrastructure without depending on a single fuel retailer.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Households, fleet depots, local governments, installers, and community energy operators coordinate generation, storage, charging windows, pricing, and maintenance through open energy management software and cooperative governance.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Smart meters, charger telemetry, inverter data, signed device logs, and utility settlement records constrain false reporting; audits and open-source control logic make dispatch and billing rules inspectable, though physical meter tampering and local governance capture remain risks.
failureModes: Permitting, interconnection queues, and utility tariff rules can slow deployment enough that gasoline retail remains more convenient.
Hardware, installation, battery replacement, and maintenance costs can erase local savings if scale and utilization are poor.
Open software does not by itself solve financing, reliability, cyber-security, or local operator competence.
adoptionPath: Start with fleet depots, schools, municipal lots, and workplaces where charging demand is predictable and fuel displacement can be measured.
Expand into neighborhood microgrids or community solar-plus-storage programs using open controls and transparent settlement.
Layer in demand response and bidirectional charging once participants trust the metering and compensation model.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The concept moves energy production and control toward households, fleets, and communities rather than centralized fuel refining and distribution.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: OpenEMS and ORES document credible coordination primitives, but real-world market settlement and governance depend heavily on local regulation and utility integration.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: The technical pieces exist, but deployment requires capital, siting, interconnection, operations, cyber-security, and customer behavior change.

incumbentPressure.rationale: The pressure is indirect but real: every locally generated and managed vehicle mile reduces long-term demand for branded petroleum retail.

disruptionConcepts.name: Open renewable hardware stack
disruptionConcepts.summary: An open specification and reference-design ecosystem for modular renewable generation, storage, and controls lets local operators assemble energy systems from interoperable parts instead of relying on closed vendor stacks or fuel supply chains.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: If renewable-energy hardware becomes more modular, repairable, and locally integrable, a portion of transportation and household energy demand can migrate away from branded liquid fuels toward locally assembled electricity systems.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: The decentralization role is open hardware and interoperable manufacturing knowledge, not a token rail; the goal is to reduce dependence on both petroleum logistics and closed energy-equipment ecosystems.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Open specifications coordinate component vendors, installers, local fabricators, standards groups, and operators around compatible interfaces, test procedures, bills of materials, and maintenance practices.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Certification test suites, open reference designs, serial-numbered components, installation records, telemetry, and third-party inspection can limit counterfeit or unsafe systems, though enforcement is much harder than in pure software.
failureModes: Safety, warranty, insurance, and grid-code compliance may keep open designs from scaling quickly.
Local fabrication may be practical for enclosures, mounts, wiring harnesses, and repairs before it is practical for high-efficiency panels, cells, inverters, or batteries.
Supply-chain concentration in advanced components can keep the stack only partially decentralized.
adoptionPath: Begin with open specifications, reference architectures, and repairable balance-of-system components for small renewable systems.
Build installer and community-lab capacity around tested designs, replacement parts, and standardized telemetry.
Use local fabrication where it is safe and cost-effective while relying on certified commercial components for high-risk power electronics and storage.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The concept pushes energy infrastructure toward modular local ownership and repair, but advanced component production remains centralized.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: LF Energy's ORES effort supports the coordination premise, but the open hardware supply chain and certification layer are still early.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: Open controls and specifications are feasible now, while broad local manufacturing of reliable energy hardware remains technically and regulatory challenging.

incumbentPressure.rationale: The concept could pressure fuel demand over long time horizons, but it is less immediate than conventional electrification and grid-scale renewable deployment.

summaryMarkdown: Prepared a new Marathon Petroleum registry bundle with one product group, two open-energy alternatives, and two disruption concepts focused on distributed energy and open renewable hardware rather than forcing a direct gasoline clone.
